title: GTA VI: Everything We Know About Rockstar's Biggest Launch Ever
excerpt: Vice City is back and bigger than ever. Here's what to expect from the most anticipated game of the decade.
author: PixelDen Staff
content: Grand Theft Auto VI is finally here — or at least, almost here. After years of leaks, speculation, and one of the most-watched trailers in gaming history, Rockstar Games is preparing to deliver what may be the most ambitious open-world game ever made.

Set in the fictional state of Leonida (a love letter to modern-day Florida), GTA VI introduces dual protagonists Lucia and her partner in a Bonnie-and-Clyde-inspired crime saga. The map is rumored to be Rockstar's largest yet, featuring a sprawling Vice City metropolis, swampy backwaters, and sun-drenched coastline.

Rockstar has confirmed an evolving online world that will expand post-launch, building on lessons learned from GTA Online's decade-long run. The economy system has been rebuilt from the ground up, with dynamic property markets and business ventures that react to player behavior across the server.

On the technical side, GTA VI leverages the RAGE engine's latest iteration with unprecedented NPC density, real-time weather systems that affect gameplay, and interior spaces that load seamlessly. Digital Foundry's early analysis suggests it may set new benchmarks for open-world fidelity.

Whether you're a returning fan or a newcomer to the series, GTA VI looks set to redefine what we expect from open-world games. Pre-orders are live now — and at R1 299.99, it's the must-have title of 2025.

title: Ghost of Yōtei: How Sucker Punch Is Reinventing the Samurai Fantasy
excerpt: A new protagonist, a new era, and a massive open world at the foot of Mount Yōtei. This sequel changes everything.
author: PixelDen Staff
content: Sucker Punch Productions stunned everyone when they revealed Ghost of Yōtei — not a direct sequel to Jin Sakai's story, but an entirely new chapter set in 1603 Hokkaido. Players step into the role of Atsu, a female warrior navigating the political turmoil of early Edo-period Japan.

The shift to Hokkaido's landscape is immediately striking. Where Tsushima offered golden forests and coastal villages, Yōtei delivers volcanic terrain, dense northern forests blanketed in snow, and hot springs that serve as natural rest points. The towering Mount Yōtei itself acts as an ever-present landmark visible from nearly every point on the map.

Combat has been significantly expanded. Atsu wields a broader arsenal than Jin, including a kusarigama (chain-sickle), a short bow for rapid shots, and sabotage tools like smoke bombs with poison tips. The stance system returns but now features a fluid stance-switching mechanic mid-combo.

Sucker Punch has also leaned into player choice this time around. Key story moments branch based on your actions, and the game tracks a reputation system where different regions may welcome or fear you depending on your approach — full ghost, honorable warrior, or something in between.
Ghost of Yōtei is shaping up to be one of the PS5's defining exclusives. If you loved Tsushima, this is the evolution you've been waiting for.

title: Monster Hunter Wilds: A Beginner's Guide to Surviving the Hunt
excerpt: New to Monster Hunter? Wilds is the perfect entry point. Here's everything you need to know before your first hunt.
author: PixelDen Staff
content: Monster Hunter Wilds has arrived, and it's already breaking records as Capcom's fastest-selling game ever. The good news? It's also the most accessible Monster Hunter to date, making it the perfect time for newcomers to jump in.

The core loop is simple: hunt monsters, carve materials, craft better gear, hunt bigger monsters. But Wilds adds seamless open-world exploration that removes the loading screens between zones. You'll ride your Seikret mount across vast biomes, tracking monsters through dynamic ecosystems where creatures interact with each other.

Weapon choice is your most important early decision. There are 14 weapon types, each playing like a completely different game. For beginners, the Sword & Shield offers great mobility and the ability to use items without sheathing. The Long Sword provides satisfying counter-based gameplay. If you want range, the Light Bowgun keeps you safely distant.

Don't neglect the new Focus Mode — it lets you target specific monster parts for precise strikes, making part-breaking more intuitive. Breaking parts not only gives you rare materials but can disable monster attacks (sever a tail, break horns, etc.).

Multiplayer is where Monster Hunter truly shines. Wilds supports seamless drop-in co-op for up to four players, and the new SOS flare system makes finding hunting partners effortless. The community is famously welcoming to new players, so don't hesitate to fire that flare.

title: Doom: The Dark Ages — Medieval Mayhem Meets Modern Carnage
excerpt: id Software takes the Doom Slayer back to his origins with shields, dragons, and a whole lot of demon-slaying.
author: PixelDen Staff
content: Doom: The Dark Ages is a prequel nobody expected and everybody needed. id Software has taken the relentless demon-shredding action of Doom Eternal and transplanted it into a dark medieval fantasy setting — and somehow it works even better than you'd think.

Set before the events of Doom 2016, The Dark Ages tells the origin story of the Doom Slayer during his time as a warrior-king fighting Hell's first invasion of a medieval world. The shift in setting brings entirely new mechanics: a shield for parrying demon attacks, a skull-crushing mace, and yes — you ride a fire-breathing dragon.

The combat rhythm has been deliberately slowed compared to Eternal's frenetic pace. id calls it 'heavy combat' — each hit feels weightier, and the new shield-parry system creates a satisfying call-and-response with enemy attacks. But make no mistake, when things escalate, The Dark Ages is just as intense as its predecessors.

Level design takes a more open approach this time. Each region is a large, explorable area with main objectives and optional challenges. Hidden secrets reward exploration with lore entries, weapon mods, and cosmetic armor sets that reference classic Doom designs.

For Xbox Game Pass subscribers, this is the flagship title of 2025. It's also available on PS5 and PC, and at 60fps across all platforms, it's a technical showcase that proves id Software remains the king of first-person combat.

title: Split Fiction Is the Best Co-op Game of 2025 (And It's Not Close)
excerpt: Hazelight does it again. Split Fiction delivers the most inventive co-op experience since It Takes Two.
author: PixelDen Staff
content: Josef Fares and Hazelight Studios have done it again. After the GOTY-winning It Takes Two, expectations were sky-high for their next co-op-only title — and Split Fiction doesn't just meet them, it vaults over them with style.

The premise is brilliantly simple: two fiction writers — Mio (sci-fi) and Zoe (fantasy) — get trapped inside a machine that's merging their stories. Each level alternates between hard sci-fi environments and lush fantasy worlds, often blending both in mind-bending ways. One moment you're platforming through a zero-gravity space station, the next you're riding a griffin through a crystal cavern.

What makes Split Fiction special is that no two levels play the same. One chapter is a top-down stealth mission, the next is a kart racer, then a rhythm game, then a full-on mech battle. Hazelight's signature approach of never repeating a mechanic is in full force, and the sheer variety is staggering.

The emotional core lands too. Mio and Zoe's friendship develops naturally through gameplay interactions, not just cutscenes. By the end, you genuinely care about these characters and the story they're writing together.

If you have a co-op partner — local or online — Split Fiction is an absolute must-play. At R749.99 with Friend's Pass (so your partner plays free), it's also one of the best values in gaming this year.
